title: Document Persist runner preference on bead and honour it during dispatch

handoff_summary.completed: Added inline documentation explaining that `retry` can pin the next dispatch to a backend, that the scheduler rehydrates a persisted runner override during dispatch, and that the override is cleared after terminal persistence.
handoff_summary.risks: Did not update broader user-facing docs because this bead's allowed scope only included the touched implementation and test files.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: Downstream work that changes retry semantics, backend selection, or finalize persistence should keep the one-shot `preferred_runner` lifecycle comments aligned with the implementation.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: Kept documentation updates inside the touched source files because the bead guardrails restricted work to the provided file scope and no standalone docs files were in scope.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: No tests were run; documentation beads do not run the test suite.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: No broader CLI or markdown documentation was updated in this pass because those files were outside the assigned scope.

conflict_risks: Downstream work that changes retry semantics, backend selection, or finalize persistence should keep the one-shot `preferred_runner` lifecycle comments aligned with the implementation.

execution_history.summary: Documented the persisted preferred-runner retry and dispatch lifecycle with concise inline comments in the scoped source files.

guardrails.template_text:
# Documentation Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Update documentation and examples that explain the assigned behavior without changing runtime feature behavior.

Allowed actions:
- Edit docs, examples, and explanatory text tied to the assigned bead.
- Focus only on files listed in the bead's touched_files and changed_files fields. Do not read or modify unrelated documentation.
- Align documentation with existing code and validated behavior.
- Identify when implementation or tests must land first and block with a handoff.

Disallowed actions:
- Change runtime behavior, production code paths, or feature logic.
- Invent undocumented behavior that is not present in the codebase.
- Approve code quality or test completeness as a substitute for review.
- Do not run the test suite. Testing is the tester agent's responsibility.

Efficiency constraints:
- Do not read the full codebase for context. Focus on the changed files and their immediate surroundings.
- Keep documentation updates concise and proportional to the change.

Expected outputs:
- Return JSON with `outcome` set to `completed` (documentation beads always complete; use `verdict` for pass/fail) and `summary` as a one-line description of what was documented.
- Return JSON with structured verdict fields for every run: `verdict`, `findings_count`, and `requires_followup`.
- Use `verdict=approved`, `findings_count=0`, and `requires_followup=false` when documentation is complete with no gaps.
- Use `verdict=needs_changes`, set `findings_count` to the number of unresolved documentation gaps, set `requires_followup=true`, and include `block_reason` when implementation must land first or documentation cannot be completed.
- Keep `completed`, `remaining`, and `risks` as concise narrative context only; they do not override the structured verdict fields.
- Accurate updated docs and clear next-agent recommendations when code changes are required first.

worker_prompt_context.title: Document Persist runner preference on bead and honour it during dispatch
worker_prompt_context.conflict_risks: Runner selection and cleanup now span both `scheduler/execution.py` and `scheduler/finalize.py`; downstream changes touching scheduler dispatch or terminal-state persistence should preserve that lifecycle coupling.

last_agent_result.summary: Documented the persisted preferred-runner retry and dispatch lifecycle with concise inline comments in the scoped source files.
